description: A delicious and popular soft fruit, raspberries are hardy and relatively easy to grow. They are categorized into summer-fruiting and autumn-fruiting varieties, providing a long harvest season from June to October. They are members of the rose family and thrive in the UK's climate.

characteristics.waterNeeds: Moderate — keep well-watered, especially during fruit swelling

pinchOutTops.info: Not typically required, but summer canes are tip-pruned in spring.

august: 🧺 Harvest summer and autumn varieties regularly

care.hardenOff: Not required for bare-root or established canes.
care.plantOut: Plant bare-root canes from Nov to Mar. Do not plant too deep; ensure the old soil mark is at the surface.
care.watering: Water at the base, especially during dry spells and fruit development. Avoid wetting the leaves to prevent grey mould.
care.feeding: Apply a general-purpose fertiliser and mulch in late February.
care.harvest: Pick when fully colored and they pull away easily from the central core.
care.overwintering: Autumn canes should be cut to the ground in Feb. Summer canes that have fruited should be cut back after harvest.

propagation.divisionMethod: Raspberries spread via underground runners (suckers). These can be dug up and replanted in autumn or winter.

deadheading.notes: Remove 'suckers' that appear outside the designated growing area to prevent spreading.

seedSaving.method: Not recommended; raspberries are best propagated via suckers.

funFact: The word 'raspberry' is derived from the Old English 'rasp', which meant 'to scrape', referring to its thorny stems.
